past_medical_history: Hypothyroidism diagnosed 2 years ago, on levothyroxine
family_history: No family history of epilepsy, psychiatric illness, or autoimmune encephalitis

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with partner and two children

neurological_exam.mental_status: Agitated and delirious. Oriented to person only. Disorganized speech with tangential and incoherent responses. Unable to follow multi-step commands. Intermittent hallucinatory behavior — appears to respond to unseen stimuli. Urinary incontinence noted.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 4 mm bilaterally, reactive to light. Extraocular movements intact but patient poorly cooperative. No facial asymmetry at rest. Intermittent involuntary lip-smacking and tongue protrusion movements observed. Gag reflex intact.
neurological_exam.motor: Moves all extremities spontaneously. Intermittent rigidity in upper extremities. Strength difficult to assess due to agitation and poor cooperation, estimated 4+/5 throughout. No focal weakness appreciated.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to pain symmetrically in all extremities. Detailed sensory exam limited by mental status.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout upper and lower extremities. Bilateral flexor plantar responses.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess due to agitation and poor cooperation.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to assess — patient bed-bound due to altered mental status and agitation.
neurological_exam.additional: Intermittent episodes of tachycardia (up to 130 bpm) and blood pressure lability (systolic ranging 100-170 mmHg) observed over monitoring period, suggestive of autonomic instability. Facial twitching noted intermittently.

patient.chief_complaint: Acute onset of disorganized speech and bizarre behavior
patient.history_present_illness: A 35-year-old woman with a history of hypothyroidism and no prior psychiatric history presented to the emergency department after being brought in by her partner due to acute onset of disorganized speech and hallucinatory behavior. Approximately three weeks prior to this presentation, she developed diffuse malaise, generalized weakness, dizziness, intermittent chills, mild shortness of breath, and headache. She was seen at an urgent care facility at that time and was advised to go to the emergency department for further workup, but she declined and returned home. Over the next two weeks, her partner noted increasing irritability, insomnia, and episodes where she appeared confused and would stare blankly for several seconds. She had difficulty concentrating at work and made uncharacteristic errors. Two days prior to presentation, she became acutely disorganized in her speech, began talking to people who were not present, and exhibited paranoid ideation, stating that her food was being poisoned. She was found wandering in the neighborhood in her nightgown and was incontinent of urine. On arrival to the ED, she was agitated, unable to provide a coherent history, and intermittently combative with nursing staff. Her partner reports no recent travel, no new medications, no known toxic exposures, and no prior episodes of similar behavior. She has no history of seizures. Her thyroid medication has been stable for over a year. The family history is notable for autoimmune thyroid disease in her mother and sister.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: Poorly organized, 6-7 Hz theta range activity replacing normal alpha rhythm

findings.frequency: 5-7 Hz theta and intermittent 2-3 Hz delta

findings.clinical_correlation: Non-specific but may indicate subcortical dysfunction or early seizure tendency

findings.morphology: Rhythmic sharp-contoured waveforms with evolution in frequency and amplitude over 45 seconds
findings.state: Single episode captured, no clear clinical correlate observed
findings.clinical_correlation: Subclinical electrographic seizure of left temporal onset — warrants anti-seizure medication and continuous EEG monitoring

artifacts.severity: Moderate — intermittently obscures underlying activity during periods of agitation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient non-cooperation
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG showing diffuse background slowing in the theta-delta range consistent with moderate encephalopathy. Intermittent rhythmic delta activity in bilateral frontotemporal regions. One subclinical electrographic seizure of left temporal onset was captured. No extreme delta brush pattern identified on this recording. Continuous EEG monitoring recommended.
eeg.limitations: Recording limited to 40 minutes. Significant muscle artifact during periods of agitation may obscure brief epileptiform discharges.
recommended_actions: Start anti-seizure medication given captured electrographic seizure
Correlate with neuroimaging and CSF findings

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Hyperintense signal along superior fronto-parietal sulci
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Mild leptomeningeal enhancement along fronto-parietal convexities

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Equivocal subtle hyperintensity in the left hippocampal region

findings.borders: Ill-defined, could represent artifact versus early inflammatory change

additional_observations: Ventricular system normal in size and configuration
Orbits, paranasal sinuses, and mastoid air cells are unremarkable
Small 4 mm pineal cyst, incidental and benign
mri.impression: Subtle FLAIR hyperintensity and mild leptomeningeal enhancement along the superior fronto-parietal sulci, suggestive of meningitis or meningoencephalitis. Equivocal subtle signal abnormality in the left mesial temporal region — cannot exclude early encephalitic process. No mass lesion, acute infarct, or hydrocephalus. Recommend clinical correlation with CSF analysis and consideration of infectious versus autoimmune etiology.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Most likely given leptomeningeal enhancement pattern
Possible — subtle temporal signal change could be consistent
Less typical — HSV usually causes more prominent temporal lobe signal abnormality and restricted diffusion

recommended_actions: Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, cultures, viral PCR, and autoimmune antibody panels
Follow-up MRI in 5-7 days if clinical course does not improve
Consider pelvic imaging if autoimmune encephalitis is suspected

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia with borderline QTc prolongation. No acute ischemic changes. The tachycardia may reflect autonomic instability, agitation, pain, or systemic illness.
ecg.clinical_correlation: QTc should be monitored if QTc-prolonging medications are administered. Tachycardia may be consistent with autonomic dysfunction in the setting of encephalitis.

CBC.clinical_significance: Leukocytosis — may reflect stress response, infection, or systemic inflammation
Neutrophilia — nonspecific, seen in infection, stress, and inflammation

BMP.clinical_significance: Mild hyponatremia — consider SIADH in the context of CNS disease
Mildly elevated — may reflect stress hyperglycemia

Thyroid.clinical_significance: Elevated TSH — suggests suboptimal thyroid replacement or Hashimoto flare. Could be red herring for Hashimoto encephalopathy.
Markedly elevated — consistent with Hashimoto thyroiditis. In the context of encephalopathy, raises differential of Hashimoto encephalopathy (SREAT).
Elevated — supports autoimmune thyroid disease. May contribute to diagnostic confusion with Hashimoto encephalopathy.

Other.clinical_significance: Normal — argues against neuroleptic malignant syndrome

labs.interpretation: Leukocytosis with neutrophilia, mild hyponatremia (consider SIADH), mildly elevated TSH with markedly elevated anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies (consistent with Hashimoto thyroiditis), mildly elevated inflammatory markers. Toxicology screen negative. Normal CK and ammonia. The combination of encephalopathy with elevated thyroid antibodies raises the differential of Hashimoto encephalopathy, but anti-NMDAR encephalitis and other autoimmune encephalitides must be excluded.

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: Present (4 bands in CSF, not matched in serum)

csf.interpretation: Lymphocytic pleocytosis with mildly elevated protein and normal glucose, consistent with aseptic meningitis or meningoencephalitis. Negative viral PCRs argue against common viral etiologies. Presence of oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index suggest intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis, raising concern for autoimmune or inflammatory CNS process. Recommend sending CSF for autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel including anti-NMDAR antibodies.

Autoimmune_Encephalitis_Panel.clinical_significance: DIAGNOSTIC: Confirms anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. CSF testing is more sensitive than serum (100% vs 85.6% sensitivity).
Supportive of diagnosis. Lower titer in serum compared to CSF is typical.

output.interpretation: CSF and serum anti-NMDAR antibodies are both positive, confirming the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. CSF titer (1:32) is higher than serum titer (1:10), consistent with intrathecal antibody production. This is the gold standard diagnostic test.

Pelvic_Imaging.value: Normal uterus and bilateral ovaries. No adnexal mass. No ovarian teratoma identified.
Pelvic_Imaging.clinical_significance: No ovarian teratoma detected. However, up to 50% of adult women with anti-NMDAR encephalitis do not have an associated teratoma. CT or MRI pelvis may be considered for further evaluation. Repeat imaging in 6 months recommended.

output.interpretation: Pelvic ultrasound negative for ovarian teratoma. Anti-NMDAR encephalitis can occur without tumor association, particularly in older patients and males. Recommend periodic surveillance imaging given ongoing risk.

Extended_Autoimmune_Encephalitis_Panel.clinical_significance: Low-titer ANA — nonspecific. May be seen in autoimmune conditions or healthy individuals. Does not indicate SLE in this context.

output.interpretation: Extended autoimmune encephalitis panel negative for LGI1, CASPR2, GABA-B, and AMPA antibodies. Low-titer ANA is a nonspecific finding. Combined with positive anti-NMDAR antibodies, the diagnosis of anti-NMDAR encephalitis is confirmed as the sole antibody-mediated process.

output.impression: CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast: No evidence of malignancy. Lungs clear, no mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Liver, spleen, pancreas, and kidneys unremarkable. No adnexal mass or pelvic pathology. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Incidental 3 mm right renal cyst, benign.
additional_observations: No ovarian teratoma or other tumor identified
Incidental 3 mm right renal cyst — benign, no follow-up needed
recommended_actions: Diagnosis of anti-NMDAR encephalitis without detectable tumor
Recommend periodic surveillance imaging for occult teratoma (every 6-12 months for 2 years)

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: Present (5 bands, not matched in serum)

output.interpretation: Repeat LP on day 7 confirms persistent lymphocytic pleocytosis and positive CSF anti-NMDAR antibodies at titer 1:32, confirming the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Oligoclonal band number has increased from 4 to 5, suggesting ongoing intrathecal immune response. CSF was specifically sent for anti-NMDAR antibodies given higher sensitivity of CSF testing (100%) compared to serum (85.6%).

output.query: Hashimoto encephalopathy vs anti-NMDAR encephalitis differential diagnosis

results.title: Steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis (SREAT): a diagnostic pitfall
results.key_finding: SREAT (Hashimoto encephalopathy) is a diagnosis of exclusion. Anti-TPO antibodies are present in 10-15% of the healthy population and do not prove causation. True SREAT should be differentiated from anti-NMDAR encephalitis by testing for neuronal surface antibodies.

results.title: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis: clinical analysis of a series and review
results.key_finding: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis is the most common autoimmune encephalitis. Characteristic progression: psychiatric symptoms, seizures, movement disorders, autonomic instability, decreased consciousness. CSF anti-NMDAR antibodies are 100% sensitive vs 85.6% for serum.

output.summary: Hashimoto encephalopathy (SREAT) and anti-NMDAR encephalitis can present similarly with encephalopathy in patients with elevated anti-thyroid antibodies. Key differentiators: (1) SREAT is a diagnosis of exclusion — always test for neuronal surface antibodies first; (2) anti-TPO positivity is common and nonspecific; (3) anti-NMDAR encephalitis has characteristic features: orofacial dyskinesias, autonomic instability, seizures, and young female predominance; (4) CSF anti-NMDAR antibody testing is the gold standard (100% sensitivity). Treatment differs: SREAT responds to steroids alone, while anti-NMDAR encephalitis requires first-line immunotherapy (IVIG + steroids) and may need second-line agents (rituximab, cyclophosphamide).

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 500 mg IV twice daily for seizure control
interactions: Levetiracetam + Levothyroxine: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Safe to co-administer.
warnings: Levetiracetam may cause behavioral side effects (irritability, agitation) — may be difficult to distinguish from encephalitis symptoms in this patient
Renal dosing adjustment needed if creatinine clearance is reduced (not required for this patient)
alternatives: Lacosamide 100 mg IV twice daily — alternative if levetiracetam behavioral effects complicate assessment
Valproic acid 500 mg IV — effective but contraindicated if patient could be pregnant

output.query: anti-NMDAR encephalitis first-line and second-line treatment protocol

results.title: Treatment and prognostic factors for long-term outcome in anti-NMDAR encephalitis: an observational cohort study
results.key_finding: First-line treatment: corticosteroids, IVIG, or plasma exchange. 53% improved with first-line therapy. Second-line (rituximab or cyclophosphamide) should be initiated within 2 weeks if no improvement. Tumor removal when applicable. 81% had good outcome at 24 months.

results.title: Clinical approach to diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis
results.key_finding: Empiric immunotherapy should be started when autoimmune encephalitis is clinically suspected, before antibody results return. Early treatment is associated with better outcomes. ICU admission for autonomic instability or hypoventilation.

output.summary: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis treatment follows a stepwise approach: First-line therapy includes high-dose IV methylprednisolone (1g/day x 5 days), IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days), or plasmapheresis. If no improvement in 2 weeks, second-line therapy with rituximab (375 mg/m2 weekly x 4) or cyclophosphamide is recommended. Tumor removal (teratoma) is critical when present. Early immunotherapy initiation correlates with better outcomes. ICU-level monitoring is often needed for autonomic instability. Recovery is typically slow (weeks to months) and relapses occur in 12-20% of cases.

differential.likelihood: Moderate — elevated anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies with known hypothyroidism make this an attractive but incorrect diagnosis. SREAT is a diagnosis of exclusion.
differential.key_distinguishing: Anti-TPO antibodies are nonspecific (10-15% of healthy population). SREAT typically lacks the movement disorders and autonomic instability seen here. Must test for neuronal surface antibodies first.

differential.likelihood: Low — considered initially but HSV PCR negative and MRI pattern not typical for HSV
differential.key_distinguishing: HSV encephalitis has temporal lobe predilection on MRI with restricted diffusion, positive CSF HSV PCR, and more acute febrile course.

differential.likelihood: Low — psychiatric symptoms prominent but accompanied by seizure, movement disorder, and autonomic instability
differential.key_distinguishing: No prodromal symptoms (avolition, social withdrawal), negative family history of psychiatric illness, presence of seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, and CSF pleocytosis all argue against primary psychiatric disease.

differential.likelihood: Very low — no antipsychotic exposure prior to symptom onset
differential.key_distinguishing: Requires exposure to dopamine-blocking agents, very high CK levels, lead-pipe rigidity, and high fever. CK is normal in this patient.

differential.likelihood: Low — no multifocal strokes on imaging
differential.key_distinguishing: Would expect vessel irregularities on angiography, multifocal infarcts, and different CSF profile.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to evaluate for structural causes of encephalopathy and assess for encephalitis pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle FLAIR hyperintensity in fronto-parietal sulci with mild leptomeningeal enhancement, suggestive of meningoencephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis to differentiate infectious vs autoimmune etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis (34 cells), oligoclonal bands present, negative viral PCRs

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for seizures and encephalopathy pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse slowing with intermittent rhythmic delta activity and subclinical electrographic seizure

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory panel including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, CK, toxicology screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis, mild hyponatremia, elevated TSH and anti-TPO antibodies — confounding findings

optimal_actions.action: Request CSF and serum anti-NMDAR antibody testing — the gold standard diagnostic test
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive CSF anti-NMDAR antibodies (titer 1:32), confirming the diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Search literature to differentiate Hashimoto encephalopathy from anti-NMDAR encephalitis given confounding thyroid antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Anti-TPO antibodies are nonspecific; must test for neuronal surface antibodies before diagnosing SREAT

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic imaging (ultrasound or CT) to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No ovarian teratoma identified — teratoma absent in up to 50% of adult cases

optimal_actions.action: Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IVIG and methylprednisolone) for presumed autoimmune encephalitis
Start anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam) given captured electrographic seizure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Levetiracetam safe with current medications, may cause behavioral effects

optimal_actions.action: Order pan-CT for paraneoplastic workup to exclude occult malignancy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No malignancy identified on CT chest/abdomen/pelvis

critical_actions: Order CSF anti-NMDAR antibody testing (gold standard, 100% sensitivity in CSF)
Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis early in workup
Do NOT accept Hashimoto encephalopathy as the diagnosis without ruling out anti-NMDAR encephalitis — anti-TPO antibodies are nonspecific
Initiate empiric immunotherapy (IVIG + methylprednisolone) when autoimmune encephalitis is suspected, before antibody results return
Start anti-seizure medication for captured electrographic seizure
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic imaging
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing Hashimoto encephalopathy based solely on elevated anti-TPO antibodies without testing for neuronal surface antibodies
Discharging patient with psychiatric diagnosis without neurological workup
Administering high-dose antipsychotics without monitoring for QTc prolongation or considering autoimmune etiology
Delaying immunotherapy while awaiting antibody results if clinical suspicion is high
Performing brain biopsy — not indicated when anti-NMDAR antibody testing is available
key_reasoning_points: Schizophrenia/primary psychosis ruled out: no prodromal symptoms (avolition, social withdrawal), no family history of psychiatric illness, presence of seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, and autonomic instability are inconsistent with primary psychiatric disorder
Substance-induced psychosis excluded: negative urine drug screen and blood alcohol, symptoms persisted and worsened in absence of substance use
Viral encephalitis (HSV) considered and excluded: negative CSF HSV PCR, MRI pattern atypical for HSV (fronto-parietal rather than temporal), lymphocytic pleocytosis without hemorrhagic features

Hashimoto encephalopathy was a strong red herring: elevated TSH, anti-TPO, and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies in a patient with known hypothyroidism. However, SREAT is a diagnosis of exclusion and anti-TPO antibodies are nonspecific (present in 10-15% of healthy individuals)
Anti-NMDAR encephalitis diagnosis confirmed by positive CSF anti-NMDAR antibodies — CSF testing is more sensitive than serum (100% vs 85.6%)

Clinical features supporting anti-NMDAR encephalitis: young woman, subacute onset, psychiatric symptoms progressing to seizures, orofacial dyskinesias (lip-smacking, tongue movements), and autonomic instability (tachycardia, BP lability)
Paraneoplastic workup (pan-CT/MRI) negative for malignancy — teratoma absent in up to 50% of adult women with anti-NMDAR encephalitis
The key diagnostic challenge is not being misled by the strongly positive thyroid antibodies into a premature diagnosis of Hashimoto encephalopathy, which would delay appropriate treatment

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty: classic anti-NMDAR encephalitis presentation with a significant red herring — markedly elevated anti-thyroid antibodies (anti-TPO 312, anti-thyroglobulin 185) in a patient with known hypothyroidism, creating a strong pull toward the incorrect diagnosis of Hashimoto encephalopathy. The agent must recognize that SREAT is a diagnosis of exclusion and specifically request anti-NMDAR antibody testing.
